speaker: interviewer
text: --Adolf Deutsch.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were marching away from this camp Buna, and people were freezing.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very cold. We used to march-- we used to hold each other, five people together. That's how we walked. So even if one was a little weaker than the other, the other one helped him somehow from both sides. So we tried to march, till we finally arrived one night. We arrived to a camp. The whole camp was lit up. There was nobody in the camp. It was a complete empty camp. What happened to the people before us, we don't know. But anyway, we stayed there. We stayed a whole night, and they didn't give us nothing to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the name of the camp where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: I cannot remember. That's-- we didn't even know the names of the camps, because every camp looked the same. We had no communications to know next town is this camp, or this camp, so we just-- we knew we arrived into the camp. The camp was empty. It was clean. There wasn't-- like nobody ever lived in that place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea what area you were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a part-- there was a part in Poland, because you could hear the-- the-- the people talking. They talked Polish, so it must have been somewheres in Poland. Because you have to remember, Buna, Auschwitz-Birkenau, that was a part of Poland. Oswiecim, that's what it used to be called. And so I know it was somewheres in Poland. And we stayed there overnight. The following morning, they woke us up early in the morning. They just kept on screaming, appel, appel. Everybody had to get up. And we stayed in the line for a couple hours, till they counted everyone, to know how many people are walking away from this camp. And we marched again, same thing as it happened the day before. And the following day, we arrived-- I remember, we arrived-- it must have been a military camp, because we stayed in the barn. And we slept on the hay. And we knew, where there is hay, there is-- it's going to be warm. And we also try to find kernels from hay. And that's what we were eating the whole night. Just like the pigeons go when they put seeds-- when you make a new ground, that's how we were doing. We were trying to find, with  the fingers, some corn, or kernels, whatever. And that's what we used to chew the whole night. Same thing, we were freezing. It was bitter cold. And the following day, we marched again. It was going on like this for about six days. Then they-- we're put on a train. We're put on the train. Already, we felt we were halfway redeemed, so we didn't have to walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a train was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was an open train. It was a cattle train. That's the only kind of trains they used those days. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And it was snowing, so we had the blankets. We were given blankets already. And so we covered ourselves with the blankets. It was really good, because we took the snow, and we ate the snow. So it helped us, at least, to survive. We were on the train. [BACKGROUND NOISE] There was-- we were put up on the train, I think, in the morning. And we were going a whole day. In the middle of the night, we arrived somewhere. It must have been a part of Czechoslovakia, because they was talking Czech. Some of the people that knew Czech, they-- they said we were somewheres in Czechoslovakia. And while we were waiting in the train, there came trucks. And they start throwing in bread into the trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Into the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Into the train. I mean, from the trucks into the train. And whoever grabbed the bread, you know, if you're lucky, you were hiding it. You could save it. If not, the other person was a little stronger than you, grabbed the bread. And you were left without it. I think I told you in the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me again.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] There was a father and a son next to me. And the father got a hold of a bread. And the son was about my age. He was a little-- still a little bit strong, stronger than the father. And he wanted the bread from the father. So he's trying to grab the bread, but the father didn't want to give away the bread. You would think a father would share with the-- with the son.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the son kept on hitting the father so hard, till he killed the father, so he can take the bread away from the father. I mean, if you think about it-- if you talk about it today, you would say how can a human being do a thing-- especially, a son to a father? Even beasts-- an animal will share with another animal.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, this was over. And we kept on going. We arrived to Bergen-Belsen. We arrived to Bergen-Belsen. There was only a few thousand people left. Most of them froze on the train. And we were so weak. It was only three kilometers from the train to the camp, but it took us a whole morning, almost like from 8:00 to 12:00, to walk, because we made step, by step, by step. That's how weak we were. And when we arrived in Bergen-Belsen, it was a tremendous large camp. I don't know if you heard about this camp. And there were a women department. There were women was separate than men. By the way, while-- while I'm talking about when we arrived to the train, there was another transport leaving Bergen-Belsen going to the train. So we used the same train. But these people were kept in a separate area where they fed these people. They looked good. They were clothed in civil clothes. They were older people, younger people, babies, children. There was one part in Bergen-Belsen kept in good condition to show for the Red Cross that people are not being abused, that people are being well taken care of. And at this transport, we found out later on, that the Satmar [? Eruv ?], the Great Chief Rebbe of Satmar. I'm sure, today, everybody knows who he was. He was in that transport, surviving in Bergen-Belsen, being transported away. Where they went from there, we never know. But it's funny how you think back to see that there were some people kept in good condition. This is how they contrived-- they-- they-- they they tricked the world, that people didn't know what was really going on. While we were in Bergen-Belsen, we didn't do nothing. There was no work. And they just kept us there with no food. We were waiting. Maybe somebody will bring a bowl of soup, a little water, something. Nothing. For a few days, there was no food. Then they started to bring in some [? gamesa ?] soup, it's called. It's made out of grass and bread. And that's what they cooked up for us, soup. And they gave us-- but it was good. It was like you say, you could-- today, you eat filet mignon. But this was only lasting a couple weeks, because-- you have to remember, [BACKGROUND NOISE] this story that I'm telling you, this happened already almost by the end of the war. So the whole country was in a turmoil. The Germans themselves didn't know what is going on, what's going to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were just waiting there. And the end was, in Bergen-Belsen, there a typhus broke out. People just died of not eating, of the stink, of the-- the-- the-- the-- the dirt. People were full of lice. People had nothing to eat, nothing to drink.

speaker: interviewee
text: People used to drink-- you'll excuse me-- the water that was running off on a puddle. People used to lay down and lick it, like-- like you see a cat goes over to a little bowl and lick-- and lick the water. That's how-- but that water was poison. And that's where people contracted the disease. People died like the flies. You just see-- you go to sleep, you never get up. And we were there a few days after this. Then, they decided to remove some of the people who were still in a condition that could be moved. We were put again on a train, and we were moved to a place called Sachsenhausen. And we were also on that train for about five days. I'm talking about-- this train, I met the man here-- he, unfortunately, is already gone. But we talked about it. He was in the same train, the same camp. And he was telling me how he survived. And I told him my-- my survival. He says, it's impossible how anybody even came out alive from that place. That was a camp-- actually, it wasn't a camp. What they did, they took those big halls where they fix airplanes. They call them--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hangars.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hangars. That's right. And they were putting thousands of peoples in one of those hangars. It was bitter cold. People could not survive. Anybody that survives from there is just-- it's like you say, a cat has nine lives. Some people had nine lives, to be able to survive. But that day that we arrived, they did bring us food to eat. So we had to stay in the line. And everybody, wherever you go, you always had with you a little bowl. That was a part of your life. Without the bowl, forget it. If you had the bowl, you get something to eat. So we always tried to hold onto that bowl. We stayed in the line to get this bowl, to get some soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if you're strong enough, you could eat it. You sit down, you eat it. But when you were weak, you sat down, some of the Ukrainians came. They were a little stronger. They took the soup away and the bowl. And then you were finished. You were left without food.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't stay long there. And we stayed, I think, about 11 days, 12 days, something like this, we stayed in that place. Then they put us again on a train. We were on the train almost a week. And it was snowing-- same-- it was winter. Whenever we-- somehow, looks like whenever we were going someplace, it was always snowing. And we were on the train with-- with a blanket. That's one thing that we tried to hold on already, because we knew, without the blanket, there's no way you can survive. We were on the train. And the only way we survived, again, is we were eating snow all the way through, till we wind up again in Bergen-Belsen. [BACKGROUND NOISE] It just looked like they didn't know what to do with the people anymore, so they brought us back to Bergen-Belsen. When we arrived in Bergen-Belsen, those people that we left a few weeks before, they were all in the streets, scattered around, dead. Some of them were in such a bad condition you couldn't touch them anymore. You had to put them on-- on a wagon to be able to move them, because they felt apart. You touched a limb, you touched an arm, it just came off, because there's nothing to hold it together. But those that they could be moved, we would put four people to each person. And we had a belt. So we used to tie a belt to a body, because we were so weak. You couldn't do it by yourself. You had to have four people to do it. Besides, a dead body's heavier than a-- a person that's alive. And we had to drag the person into the barracks. We had special barracks where they threw one dead body after another, one on top of the other. Just like you take wood. You pile wood one on top of the other, that's how we piled these, all dead bodies. After doing this for a few days, and I just kept on seeing this. There's no end. Nobody's going to come out of this alive. We were nothing but skeletons. We had just about enough energy to be able to make a step. Besides, we have to work yet. We had to pull these dead people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after the four day, I decided-- I pulled in a dead person. Instead to go away, I laid down between the dead people. And I stayed there two and a half days between the dead people. That was the last two and a half days before we were liberated by the English. That's how I survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: If I wouldn't have done this, probably, two days later, they would have pulled me into that-- that pile of-- of dead people. That was the only way I really survived. And I was so weak, when the English came in, they kept on searching for live people. And they heard me screaming, hollering, you know, call for help. And that's how they took me away, in an ambulance. And they took me to a city where there was some hospitals. And I was in that hospital for six weeks. There was a hospital with nuns, sisters. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And I was treated just like a little baby. They washed me and bathed me, put me in a clean bed, turned me over, put me over to the other side. Because you were so weak, you couldn't even turn over from one side to another. They fed me, spoon fed, because there was nothing. Everything was dried up. It was a lucky thing that I didn't eat anything in the camps, because unfortunately, when the English gave food in the camps, they give condensed milk and the-- the-- the ham that's rolled on papers in those-- in those pushkas, in those-- those cans. And whoever ate that stuff just died. The following day, whoever drank or ate did not come out alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in a way, I was lucky that I didn't eat or I didn't drink, that they took me right away into a hospital. And I was there for six weeks in that hospital, till I got well. They really took care of it like a little baby. They fed me. They gave me to drink with a-- with a-- with a straw.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was-- I'm thinking back many a times how a person really can survive. If they really take care of you, they feed you, and they give you good food, they can bring you back to life. I was, actually, I would say, 99% dead. Maybe 1%, I still kept myself [NON-ENGLISH] still alive in me. And after the six weeks, they told me, you have to leave. I didn't want to leave. I had it so good there, [LAUGHS] that I didn't feel like leaving. But the doctor says, you must leave. You have to make room for others. There are other sick people. They need the bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they told me to leave the hospital. They gave me some clothes. And I went away from there. Where do you go? It was just like you let a bird out of a cage. Where was the bird supposed to go? So I stood there for a little while. That particular area where the hospital was, there was some Gypsies with wagons. So I went over to the Gypsies. Some of them were Germans Gypsies. Some of them were Hungarian Gypsies-- survivors. They were all in the camps, but they survived somehow in a different way. So I kept on talking to them. Is there any other Jews? Nobody knew. But I kept on going into the city, and I kept on asking questions. I asked, I kept on asking, till I found another man. The only way we recognized each other, we used to call each other [NON-ENGLISH]. The word [NON-ENGLISH] means you're a Jew. In other words, you're my-- my own. You're a brother. And so I asked him, what do you do here? Where do you go here? He says, there's one place. He told me address. And that's where all the refugees gather. When you get there, you sign in. There are books. And you sign in. In every book that there is, put your name down. If someone is looking for someone, or if you are looking for someone, look-- go through the books. You'll see who is alive, who survived. So I put my name in. There was a man next to me. He saw my name, Deutsch. So he says, well, I know a Deutsch. I says, who do you know? Says he knows a lady by the name Adieu Deutsch. Adieu Deutsch, first, I thought was my sister. Meanwhile, I found out it was my first cousin, my uncle's daughter. So where do you go? Where-- where is she? She is in the camp in Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewee
text: See, Bergen-Belsen was a camp for-- for the Jews. And there was a camp where they kept the military, the soldiers, the officers. They had regular barracks, good barracks, good buildings. So the people from the old barracks moved into these barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my-- my cousin, she was in one of the rooms with five other girls. But how do you get there? There was no trains. There was no cars. There was no trucks. The only way you had communications over there is bicycles. [BACKGROUND NOISE] It just so happened that I knew how to use a bicycle. I had one in Europe. Where do you get a bike? So I watched a lady going into a store. And I waited about five minutes. I see she's not coming out. I took the bike, and I went away. And I found out how to get to the camps, so I came into the camps. But how do you find this-- this lady by the name of Adieu Deutsch? So there were special blocks with buildings that were only women. There were blocks for buildings only for men. So I kept on going from one room to another, one building to another, till I found this lady by the name Adieu Deutsch. She wound up to be my wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Through the man-- this man happened to be a man from her town, knew her parents and knew her. This is how I happened to-- it's just-- it's interesting how it happens. He happened to sit next to me. I put my name in the book. And through him, I found out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so we stayed in the camp. But you need money. How do you make money? So me and a friend of mine, we came up with an idea. See, those days, the trains had no conductors. The trains was just going from one to another without billetten, without any tickets.

speaker: interviewee
text: You hopped on a train, like, today, the hobos, and you go wherever the train goes. But there were some Germans also travelling on those trains, and they all had luggages. We went empty-handed. And we waited till it turns dark, till the train goes a few hours. And you know, when you go on a train, you're bound to fall asleep. And we watched those-- some of the Germans. They fell asleep. And we knew which was their luggage, so we took the luggage first. We went from that cabin. We went towards the end. When the train slowed down, we jumped off. And that's how we organized-- we started to organize to have something. And the first luggage that I found was a piece of material to make a coat. I had no coat. But my wife-- now that I can talk about she was my-- my-- my cousin-- she only had a-- a-- a blanket. So I figured she was a fancy girl, home-- from a rich home. I think I'm going to make her a coat. That was the first coat [LAUGHS] that I organized myself from the material that I had stolen from one of the Germans [LAUGHS] in a piece of luggage. And this, we continued doing every day. We jumped off the trains. We waited until another train comes on. Because before you go on a hill, train slows down. So we used to jump on the train, and we used to come back. This is how we did it for almost five weeks. Every single day, we used to go and steal luggages.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some luggages, we were lucky. Some luggages, we found nothing but papers. You know? We couldn't do nothing. [LAUGHS] So depends how lucky we were. And today, you look at the youngsters, they have nothing else to do. They have no money. They do the same thing 50 years later. They broke in-- break into a house, they steal things to be able to buy cigarettes, to go and play the-- the machines, you know? And we needed money for survival. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was difficult times. I was young that time. I was already almost 19, 18 and 1/2, 19 years old. And this is what we did for a while. And after that, things start normalize a little bit more in the camps. And the Joint-- that's a Jewish organization-- they started to give out clothes, started to give out shoes. They gave us better food already in the camps. And they started to organize businesses. People opened up a little bakery, if you were a baker. If you were a merchant, you were selling stuff before, you started to handle. You started to make business. And then, we started to deal with coffee, with cocoa, with cigarettes, liquor. This was all on the-- on the black market. [BACKGROUND NOISE] You couldn't buy in a regular store, because the military used to get that stuff. And somehow, it wound up in the camps. In the camps, they started to make black markets. So we used to take this stuff, we used to go-- now, later on, we already had trucks. I mean, not our own. We used to rent someone's truck, and we used to go. And we used to go into the cities. We used to sell the stuff to make some money, till the Germans started to clamp down on the black market. That's when we stopped. We stayed in the camps. And I waited till I got the papers to come to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1949. I had-- actually, I had two uncles that time that I waited for the papers. One lived in California, one lived in New York. Whichever one's going to come first, that's the one I'm going to go to. So I happened to get the papers from New York. The uncle from New York sent me the papers to come to the United States. But I had to go, first, in-- into a quarantine for two weeks, before they let us out from Germany. Because at that time, the Americans didn't let in anybody that was sick. You had to be in a good condition. And someone had to guarantee for you that I will not be a burden on the government, that someone will support me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had my uncle. He vouched-- he vouched for me, that he's going to see to it that I'm-- and it's still-- I never was an unemployed in the United States. I never collected one cent in the United States, unemployment. I was only here one week in the United States, and I found myself a job.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Joint provided us with a run down hotel, till they're going to find us somewhere suitable to live. Excuse me. But I found out myself, on my own, found myself a job. After that, I got my uncle's apartment, because it was already too small for him. So we got the apartment. This is how we started. We established ourselves in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out what happened with your sisters and brothers who survived? How were you reunited?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1946, after I married my wife in the camps, my-- one of my brothers-- I was going to the train. While we were on the train, he heard my voice. You know, we have a special voice, all my brothers. We all talk the same voice. You could be between a million people, you could recognize this voice. And he heard my voice, and he kept on screaming my name. But-- but there were so many people at the train. Everybody's trying to go on on those trains. You can imagine, for free, so everybody was pushing. He just arrived with another train, and we were supposed to leave with the same train. When he heard my voice, he kept on screaming my name. And we kept on coming closer to each other, till we found each other. So instead to go away, I came back. And he stayed with me in the camps, in-- in-- in-- in Bergen-Belsen. While he was in the camp, we decided to go back home, to go back to Romania. Through him, I found out that my older two brothers survived. They were in the Russian-- by the Russian prisoners. So after the war, the Russians let them go home. So I heard that two of my older brothers survived. When we were in Romania, we were in a city, I discovered that my oldest sister survived with her family. And we stayed in that city a few days. Then, I went back to my city where I was born. When I arrived to that city, I discovered I have three more sisters survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want me to stop?

speaker: interviewer
text: If you want to stop, you can stop.
